title: Human relationships: social responsibility
summary: Social responsibility asks a deceptively simple question: when will a person help another, and when will they stand by? This lesson builds the two halves of the answer. First, why we help at all — the biological/evolutionary, cognitive and sociocultural explanations of prosocial behaviour and altruism. Second, why we sometimes do not — bystanderism and the bystander effect, driven by diffusion of responsibility and pluralistic ignorance (Latané & Darley). Every explanation is anchored to a named study with aim, procedure and findings, and everything is aimed at the one skill Paper 2 rewards: not describing research, but USING it to answer the question.
learningObjectives: Define prosocial behaviour and altruism precisely, and distinguish them.
Explain prosocial behaviour and altruism using biological/evolutionary, cognitive and sociocultural explanations, each supported by a named study.
Explain the bystander effect and the factors behind it — diffusion of responsibility and pluralistic ignorance — using Latané and Darley's research.
Evaluate the research on prosocial behaviour and bystanderism (methodology, ethics, ecological validity, generalisability).
Analyse strategies for promoting prosocial behaviour, and USE research to answer a Paper 2 essay to a top-band standard.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: To Help, or to Stand By?
simpleExplanation.summary: This topic has two sides. One side explains why humans help each other — through evolution, through empathy, and through culture. The other side explains a disturbing finding: the more people who witness an emergency, the LESS likely any one of them is to help. Your job in the exam is not just to know both sides, but to weave studies into an argument.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a passenger slumped on a crowded train. On a busy carriage, each person thinks 'someone else will deal with it' (responsibility spreads out) and 'no one else looks worried, so maybe it's fine' (everyone misreads everyone else). Now picture the same passenger with only YOU in the carriage. The responsibility is entirely yours and there is no one else's calm face to copy — so you act. That shift in perceived responsibility and social cues is the heart of bystanderism.
steps: Define precisely: prosocial behaviour (any act intended to benefit another) vs altruism (helping with no expected personal gain, possibly at a cost). Getting this distinction right sets up the whole essay.
Explain WHY we help across three levels: biological/evolutionary (kin selection), cognitive (empathy-altruism, cost-reward), sociocultural (norms, in-group).
Explain WHY we sometimes don't: the bystander effect, via diffusion of responsibility and pluralistic ignorance (Latané & Darley).
Then do the exam skill: pick two studies, explain them fully, and USE them — link each finding back to the exact claim you are making, and evaluate.

flashcards.back: Any behaviour intended to benefit another person, a group, or society. The motive may be selfish (reward, approval, relief of distress) OR selfless — the term itself says nothing about motive.

A specific type of prosocial behaviour where the helper expects no personal gain and may even pay a cost. The debated question is whether 'pure' altruism exists or whether all helping is ultimately egoistic.
The finding that an individual is LESS likely to help in an emergency as the number of other bystanders increases. It is not apathy — a bystander may be highly distressed yet still fail to act.

As more bystanders are present, the personal responsibility to act is spread across the group, so each individual feels less compelled to intervene ('someone else will'). Affects step 3 of Latané & Darley's model — assuming responsibility.

In an ambiguous situation people look to others for cues; if everyone else appears calm, each privately concludes it is not an emergency — even though all of them are privately worried. Affects step 2 — interpreting the event.

Diffusion of responsibility = 'someone else will act' (about WHO is responsible). Pluralistic ignorance = 'no one looks worried, so it must be fine' (about WHETHER it is an emergency). Different steps, different mechanisms — do not merge them.

We are predisposed to help genetic relatives, because aiding shared genes' survival is favoured by natural selection. Supported by Madsen et al. (2007): participants endured more discomfort to earn money for closer relatives.
Cognitive theory: feeling empathy for someone in need produces genuinely altruistic motivation to help; without empathy, helping depends on an egoistic cost-benefit calculation.
Witnessing an emergency creates unpleasant physiological arousal; the bystander is motivated to reduce it and weighs the costs of helping against the costs of not helping before acting.
Five steps to intervention: 1) notice the event, 2) interpret it as an emergency, 3) assume responsibility, 4) know how to help, 5) implement help. Failure at ANY step means no help.

Cultural norms (e.g. reciprocity, collectivism) and social identity shape helping: people help perceived in-group members more readily because the in-group's welfare is tied to their own social identity.

flashcards.front: Exam skill: describe vs USE research
flashcards.back: Describing = stating a study's procedure and findings. USING = linking the finding explicitly to the claim it supports and to the question. Criterion C rewards USE, not description.

quickCheck.prompt: A witness thinks 'there are twenty other people here — one of them will surely call for help.' This is an example of:
In a group where everyone privately feels alarmed but each stays calm because no one else looks worried, and so all conclude it is not an emergency, the mechanism is:
The bystander effect predicts that as the number of bystanders increases, the probability that any one of them helps:
Which explanation of prosocial behaviour is BIOLOGICAL/evolutionary rather than cognitive or sociocultural?
An essay writes: 'Piliavin (1969) staged a collapse on a subway; the ill victim was helped more than the drunk victim.' To move from DESCRIBING to USING this study, the student must next:
options: Add the exact percentages for both conditions
Name the other researchers on the study
Explicitly link the finding to the claim — that perceived cost of helping shapes intervention — and to the question
State the year the study was published
quickCheck.answer: Explicitly link the finding to the claim — that perceived cost of helping shapes intervention — and to the question

faq.q: What is the difference between altruism and prosocial behaviour?
faq.a: Prosocial behaviour is the umbrella term for any act intended to benefit others, whatever the motive. Altruism is a narrower subset: helping with no expectation of personal gain, possibly at a personal cost. The dividing line is motive — prosocial behaviour can be egoistic OR altruistic, whereas altruism is by definition selfless. Blurring the two is a frequent way to lose knowledge (criterion B) marks.

faq.q: Is the bystander effect the same as apathy?
faq.a: No. Apathy is not caring. The bystander effect is a social phenomenon in which the presence of others psychologically inhibits an otherwise concerned person from acting. Latané and Darley's key contribution was showing that non-intervention is driven by situational mechanisms — diffusion of responsibility and pluralistic ignorance — not by a lack of feeling. In their studies, participants who failed to act often showed clear distress.

faq.q: How do I turn a study I have memorised into essay marks?
faq.a: Do not narrate it. State the claim first ('the presence of others reduces helping through diffusion of responsibility'), then bring in the study's aim, procedure and findings, then link the specific finding back to that claim AND to the question, then evaluate it (methodology, ethics, validity, generalisability). That describe-then-USE-then-evaluate loop is exactly what criteria C and D reward — and it is what our marking engine looks for.

sections.content: In March 1964 the murder of Kitty Genovese in New York was reported — accurately or not — as an event witnessed by many who failed to intervene. Whatever the truth of that particular case, it launched one of social psychology's most productive research programmes: why do people sometimes fail to help those in obvious need? This lesson covers both sides of social responsibility — the forces that push us to help (prosocial behaviour and altruism) and the forces that hold us back (bystanderism) — and trains the one skill Paper 2 actually rewards: USING research inside an argument rather than merely retelling it.
Defining prosocial behaviour and altruism

Prosocial behaviour is a broad category: any action intended to benefit another person, a group, or society — donating, volunteering, comforting, rescuing. Crucially, the term is silent about motive. A person may help to gain approval, to discharge a sense of duty, or simply to relieve their own discomfort at watching someone suffer. Altruism is the narrower, more contested idea: prosocial behaviour performed with no expectation of personal gain, and sometimes at a genuine cost to the helper. Whether 'pure' altruism exists at all — or whether every helpful act conceals some self-interest — is one of the central debates in this topic, and a debate you can exploit for critical-thinking marks.

items: **Prosocial behaviour** = any act intended to benefit another; motive may be selfish or selfless.
**Altruism** = a subset of prosocial behaviour with no expected personal gain, possibly at a cost.
The live question — does pure altruism exist, or is all helping ultimately egoistic? — is a ready-made evaluation point.

sections.content: Why we help: three levels of explanation

Explanations for prosocial behaviour sit at the biological, cognitive and sociocultural levels. A strong essay does not have to cover all three, but it must anchor each explanation it uses to a named study — and then use that study, not just cite it.

items: **Biological / evolutionary — kin selection.** We are predisposed to help genetic relatives because helping shared genes survive is favoured by natural selection. Helping is therefore expected to increase with genetic relatedness.

**Cognitive — empathy-altruism (Batson).** Whether helping is altruistic depends on emotion: feeling empathy for the person in need triggers genuinely altruistic motivation; without empathy, helping is decided by an egoistic cost-benefit calculation.

**Cognitive — arousal: cost-reward (Piliavin).** An emergency produces unpleasant arousal; the bystander is motivated to reduce it and weighs the costs of helping (danger, effort, disgust) against the costs of not helping (guilt, blame).

**Sociocultural — norms and identity.** Cultural norms (reciprocity, social responsibility) and social identity shape helping. People help perceived in-group members more readily because the in-group's welfare is tied to their own social identity and self-esteem.

sections.content: Supporting study 1 — Madsen et al. (2007): kin selection

**Aim:** to test whether willingness to help increases with genetic relatedness, as kin selection predicts. **Procedure:** participants held a painful, physically demanding isometric position (a 'ski-squat' against a wall); the longer they held it, the more money was earned for a specified beneficiary who varied in relatedness — from a close relative (parent, sibling) through cousins to non-relatives such as a charity. **Findings:** participants endured the discomfort for longer as the beneficiary's genetic relatedness increased, and the pattern replicated across cultural samples. **Use it like this:** the graded relationship between effort and relatedness is exactly what kin selection predicts — cost tolerated tracks shared genes — so the study supports a biological account of altruism. **Evaluation:** the controlled effort measure is a strength, but the artificial task has low ecological validity, and cultural learning about family obligation is a confound that a purely genetic reading ignores.
Supporting study 2 — Batson et al. (1981): empathy-altruism

**Aim:** to test whether empathy produces genuinely altruistic helping, independent of self-interest. **Procedure:** participants watched 'Elaine' apparently receive electric shocks. Two variables were manipulated: empathy (high — told Elaine shared their values; low — told she was dissimilar) and ease of escape (easy — free to leave after two shocks; difficult — had to watch all ten). Participants were then offered the chance to take Elaine's remaining shocks. **Findings:** in the high-empathy condition most helped whether escape was easy or difficult; in the low-empathy condition helping dropped sharply when escape was easy. **Use it like this:** if helping were always egoistic, low-empathy participants would leave whenever they could — and they did; but high-empathy participants helped even with an easy exit, which self-interest cannot explain, supporting a genuinely altruistic motive. **Evaluation:** an elegant 2×2 design that isolates motive, but the lab task is artificial and the deception raises ethical concerns, limiting how far the 'altruism' generalises beyond the setting.

sections.question: Explain one theory of prosocial behaviour with reference to one study. [9]
sections.solution:
One cognitive theory of prosocial behaviour is Batson's empathy-altruism model (1981). It proposes that whether helping is altruistic depends on the emotion the situation evokes. If a person feels EMPATHY — taking the perspective of the one in need and sharing their distress — they are motivated to help for genuinely altruistic reasons, regardless of personal reward. If empathy is absent, helping is decided by an egoistic cost-benefit analysis: the person helps only when the personal costs of NOT helping (guilt, disapproval) outweigh the costs of helping.

Batson et al. (1981) tested this. The aim was to separate altruistic from egoistic motivation. Female participants watched 'Elaine' apparently receive electric shocks, and two variables were manipulated: empathy (high vs low, by describing Elaine as similar or dissimilar to the participant) and ease of escape (easy — leave after two shocks; difficult — watch all ten). Participants were then offered the chance to take the remaining shocks themselves.

The findings map directly onto the theory. In the high-empathy condition, most participants helped whether escape was easy or difficult — empathy produced helping even when self-interest offered an easy way out, which is the signature of altruistic motivation. In the low-empathy condition, helping fell when escape was easy: with no empathy, participants simply left, revealing an egoistic motive of reducing their own discomfort. The link is precise: the ONE condition self-interest cannot explain — helping despite an easy escape — is exactly the condition empathy was predicted to produce, so the study supports the model's central claim.

sections.content: Notice what makes the answer above worth full marks: after stating each finding it says WHY that finding supports the theory ('the one condition self-interest cannot explain...'). That is the difference between describing a study and USING it. A 9-mark 'explain' answer that merely narrates Batson's procedure and results, without linking them back to the model, sits a whole band lower.
Why we sometimes don't help: bystanderism

The bystander effect is the counter-intuitive finding that the presence of others REDUCES the likelihood that any individual helps in an emergency. Latané and Darley argued that failing to help is not apathy but the outcome of a cognitive decision process shaped by the social situation. Their decision model sets out five steps to intervention — notice the event, interpret it as an emergency, assume responsibility, know how to help, implement help — and failure at any single step stops helping. Two mechanisms explain most failures.

items: **Pluralistic ignorance** operates at step 2 (interpretation). In an ambiguous situation people read others' reactions as information. If everyone else looks calm, each privately concludes it is not an emergency — even though all of them are secretly worried. The group collectively misreads itself into inaction.

**Diffusion of responsibility** operates at step 3 (assuming responsibility). The more people present, the more thinly personal responsibility is spread, so each individual feels less obliged to act — 'someone else will'.

These are DIFFERENT mechanisms: pluralistic ignorance is about WHETHER there is an emergency; diffusion of responsibility is about WHO must deal with it. Weak essays merge them; strong essays keep them separate and pin each to the right step.

sections.content: Supporting study 3 — Latané & Darley (1968): the smoke-filled room

**Aim:** to test whether the presence of others reduces the tendency to react to a possible emergency (pluralistic ignorance). **Procedure:** participants completed a questionnaire either alone, with two other genuine participants, or with two passive confederates who deliberately ignored the situation, while smoke was pumped into the room. **Findings:** 75% of participants who were alone reported the smoke, but only 38% did so in groups of three, and reporting fell further when the two others were passive confederates. Many in the group conditions later said they had decided the smoke was steam or a harmless fault. **Use it like this:** because participants downgraded a genuine hazard to a non-emergency by reading others' calm as evidence, the study directly demonstrates pluralistic ignorance shaping interpretation (step 2). **Evaluation:** a controlled manipulation of group size, but an artificial task with low ecological validity, and deceiving participants into believing they might be in danger raises ethical concerns about psychological harm.
Supporting study 4 — Latané & Darley (1968): the seizure (intercom) study

**Aim:** to test whether increasing the number of perceived bystanders reduces helping through diffusion of responsibility. **Procedure:** participants sat in individual cubicles for what they believed was a group discussion over an intercom; group size was varied so each thought they were one of two, three, or six people. A confederate then staged an apparent epileptic seizure over the intercom; the measure was whether and how fast the participant left to seek help. **Findings:** when participants believed they alone heard the victim, 85% helped; when they believed four others were also present, only 31% helped, and those who did help were slower. **Use it like this:** since the ONLY thing varied was the perceived number of others, the drop in helping isolates diffusion of responsibility as the cause (step 3). **Evaluation:** strong internal validity from tight control, but low ecological validity and clear ethical costs — participants believed a person was seizing and showed real distress.

sections.question: Discuss bystanderism and/or factors influencing prosocial behaviour. [22]
sections.solution:
**MODEL ESSAY**

**Thesis (introduction).** Whether a person helps another in an emergency is determined less by their character than by the social situation they are in. This essay argues that the bystander effect — the finding that the presence of others reduces the likelihood of helping — is best explained by two situational mechanisms, diffusion of responsibility and pluralistic ignorance, as set out in Latané and Darley's decision model. It will develop this claim using two studies, evaluate how convincingly each supports it, and conclude that the situational account is powerful but incomplete without motivational factors such as the perceived cost of helping.

**Knowledge — the model.** Latané and Darley (1970) proposed that helping requires passing through five steps: noticing the event, interpreting it as an emergency, assuming responsibility, knowing how to help, and implementing help. Failure at any step ends helping. Two mechanisms explain the most common failures. Pluralistic ignorance strikes at step 2: in an ambiguous situation people infer whether it is a genuine emergency from others' reactions, so if everyone appears calm, each privately concludes there is no emergency — even while all are secretly alarmed. Diffusion of responsibility strikes at step 3: as more bystanders are present, personal responsibility is spread across the group, so each individual feels less obliged to act. These mechanisms are distinct — one concerns whether an emergency exists, the other concerns who must respond — and keeping them separate is essential to a precise argument.

**Study 1 — used, not just described.** The seizure (intercom) study (Latané & Darley, 1968) tested diffusion of responsibility. Participants believed they were in a group discussion over an intercom while sitting alone in cubicles; the perceived group size was varied. A confederate staged an epileptic seizure, and the researchers recorded whether and how quickly participants sought help. When participants believed they were the only listener, 85% helped; when they believed four others were also present, only 31% did, and more slowly. Because the ONLY variable manipulated was the perceived number of bystanders, the fall in helping cannot be explained by the victim, the emergency, or the participants' personalities — it isolates diffusion of responsibility as the cause. This is the study doing argumentative work: it does not merely illustrate the concept, it rules out rival explanations for the drop in helping.

**Study 2 — used, not just described.** The smoke-filled room study (Latané & Darley, 1968) tested pluralistic ignorance. Participants completed a questionnaire alone, in genuine groups of three, or with two passive confederates, while smoke seeped into the room. 75% of lone participants reported the smoke; only 38% did in groups; reporting fell further with passive confederates. Participants who stayed silent later reported reinterpreting the smoke as steam or a fault. This finding is used to support the argument's second mechanism: interpretation itself was distorted by others' inaction, so the failure occurred at step 2, before responsibility was ever considered. Placed beside Study 1, the two studies show the two mechanisms operating at two different steps of the same model — jointly, not redundantly.

**Developed critical thinking.** Three lines of evaluation strengthen and qualify the argument. (1) Methodology: both studies achieve high internal validity through tight control — the seizure study's single manipulated variable is what licenses the causal claim — but both are artificial laboratory set-ups with low ecological validity; a smoke-filled room is not a street assault. Piliavin et al.'s (1969) subway field study, where an apparently ill victim was helped far more than an apparently drunk one, shows helping in a real setting and points to a further factor the situational model underplays: the perceived COST of helping. This does not overturn Latané and Darley but shows the situational account needs a motivational supplement. (2) Ethics: both studies deceived participants and exposed them to distress (believing a person was seizing, or that they might be in danger), so the evidence for the model carries an ethical cost that must temper confidence in replicating it. (3) Construct validity: the two mechanisms are hard to separate cleanly in the field, where interpretation and responsibility interact — a limitation the controlled designs paper over rather than resolve. Notice that each evaluation point is tied back to the question of how well the situational account explains bystanderism, not listed generically.

**Conclusion.** The evidence supports a clear answer: bystanderism is primarily situational. Latané and Darley's model, and the two studies used above, show that ordinary people fail to help not through apathy but because responsibility diffuses and ambiguous situations are collectively misread. Yet the field evidence on cost-reward shows the situational account is necessary but not sufficient — motivation matters too. The most defensible position is that whether we help is decided by an interaction of the situation (how many others, how ambiguous) and the perceived costs of acting, rather than by helpfulness as a fixed trait.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 2 essay, out of 22):**

- **A — Focus on the question (2/2).** The introduction states a clear thesis that directly answers the command term 'discuss' and names the factors to be argued. Every paragraph refers back to that thesis, and the conclusion answers the question rather than just summarising. Top band: the question is understood and the answer is focused throughout.
- **B — Knowledge and understanding (6/6).** The decision model, both mechanisms, and four relevant concepts are explained accurately and with the right psychological terminology, and the distinction between diffusion of responsibility and pluralistic ignorance is made explicitly. Top band: knowledge is accurate, relevant and detailed.
- **C — Use of research (6/6).** Two studies are described with aim, procedure and findings AND then explicitly used — each finding is linked to the specific claim it supports, and Study 1 is shown ruling out rival explanations. The research supports the argument rather than sitting beside it. Top band: research is used effectively to support the answer.
- **D — Critical thinking (6/6).** Evaluation is developed, not listed: methodology, ethics and construct validity are each connected back to how well the situational account explains bystanderism, and a contrasting field study is used to qualify the thesis. Top band: critical thinking is relevant, developed and consistent.
- **E — Clarity and organisation (2/2).** The essay is organised (thesis → model → two studies → evaluation → conclusion), ideas are clearly expressed, and studies are referenced consistently. Top band: clear and well organised.

**Total: 22/22.** The single move that lifts C and D into the top band is the same one throughout: research and evaluation are USED to answer the question, never merely reported.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this [22]-mark question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same Paper 2 essay markband (A Focus /2, B Knowledge /6, C Use of research /6, D Critical thinking /6, E Clarity /2), telling you the band you reached on each criterion and why. Watch for the engine's two caps: (1) if studies are only described and not linked to your argument, criterion C is capped at the 3–4 band no matter how much you write; (2) if your critical thinking is generic or listed rather than tied to the question, criterion D is capped at the 3–4 band. The habit that beats both caps: after every study, add one sentence that says why it supports your point — and after every evaluation, add one sentence that ties it back to the question.
Promoting prosocial behaviour

Understanding what blocks helping tells us how to remove the blocks. Because bystanderism runs through Latané and Darley's five steps, effective strategies target specific steps. To defeat pluralistic ignorance (step 2), make the emergency unambiguous — a clear cry of 'She's having a heart attack — this is an emergency!' removes the interpretive doubt others' calm faces create. To defeat diffusion of responsibility (step 3), assign responsibility to a named individual — 'You in the red jacket, call an ambulance now' — so it can no longer be spread across the crowd. More broadly, research shows that simply teaching people about the bystander effect makes them more likely to help later, because knowing the mechanisms lets people recognise and override them; and modelling prosocial behaviour, plus fostering an inclusive in-group identity so more people count as 'us', both raise helping. Each strategy is strongest in the exam when tied back to the mechanism it counteracts.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing diffusion of responsibility with pluralistic ignorance** — one is about WHO acts ('someone else will'), the other about WHETHER it is an emergency ('no one looks worried, so it's fine'). Merging them loses both knowledge (B) and precision marks.
**Treating bystanderism as apathy** — the whole point of Latané and Darley's work is that non-helpers are often distressed but situationally inhibited. Calling it 'not caring' misstates the theory.
**Blurring altruism and prosocial behaviour** — prosocial behaviour can be egoistic; altruism by definition is not. Define both precisely before you argue.
**Describing studies instead of USING them** — narrating a procedure and result without linking the finding to your claim caps criterion C. Always add the 'this supports my point because…' sentence.

**Listing evaluation instead of developing it** — a string of generic strengths and limitations not connected to the question caps criterion D. Tie every evaluation point back to how well the explanation answers the question.

**Answering only half a 'discuss' question** — a [22] essay must build and weigh an argument (thesis, evidence, evaluation, conclusion), not just describe factors. A purely descriptive answer cannot reach the top bands even if the facts are correct.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 essay marked: Discuss bystanderism and/or factors influencing prosocial behaviour. [22]
